title: Pig notifiable diseases in the UK
subtitle: What to look for and what to do if you suspect a listed disease.
excerpt: Seven pig diseases are notifiable in the UK. If you suspect one, you must contact APHA before calling your vet, before moving any animals, and before making any changes to the holding. Most smallholders will never encounter these diseases, but knowing the signs and the reporting duty means the response is correct if it ever happens.
sourceNotes: APHA list of notifiable pig diseases, gov.uk. DEFRA Pig disease surveillance reports. Pig Health and Welfare Council (PHWC) guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: A disease that must by law be reported to the relevant government authority as soon as it is suspected. Failure to report a notifiable disease is a criminal offence.
Animal and Plant Health Agency — the UK government body responsible for animal disease surveillance and control. First point of contact for suspected notifiable disease.

content.text: on 03000 200 301 right away. You must call even if the diagnosis is not confirmed. Suspicion alone is enough to trigger the duty. Moving animals, giving treatment, or changing anything on the premises without APHA permission can harm an outbreak investigation.
Foot and Mouth Disease (FMD). High fever, blistering on the snout, feet, and teats. Most feared outbreak disease in UK livestock. Last UK outbreak in 2001.
Classical Swine Fever (CSF). High fever, bluish discolouration of the skin, neurological signs, high mortality. Not present in the UK but a risk from imported pigs or feed.
African Swine Fever (ASF). Similar signs to CSF. Present in Europe and spreading. No vaccine or treatment.
Swine Vesicular Disease. Blisters on feet and snout, clinically the same as FMD. Lab testing is needed to tell them apart.
Teschen Disease (Enterovirus encephalomyelitis). Rare. Neurological signs, paralysis.
Aujesky's Disease (Pseudorabies). Neurological signs. UK has been disease-free since 2004.
Vesicular Stomatitis. Blisters on the mouth, feet, and teats. Very rare in UK.

Call APHA right away if you see any of these signs. Blisters or sores on the snout, mouth, or feet. High fever and the pig cannot stand. Several animals dying in a short period. Any pig that is circling, falling over, or unable to stand when it was healthy before.

Feeding kitchen scraps or catering waste to pigs is illegal in the UK. The ban exists because the 2001 FMD outbreak was traced to a farm feeding restaurant waste. Dry farm-produced cereals, vegetables grown on the holding, and commercially prepared pig feed are all legal. If in doubt about any ingredient, don't feed it.

A small closed herd has a low risk if basic biosecurity is in place. No kitchen scraps or catering waste. No contact with feral pigs or wild boar. New stock in quarantine for fourteen days before mixing. Visitors disinfect boots. Most notifiable diseases arrive via animals or feed, not through the air.
